explanation: Proud, flat, shiny amalgams usually mean surrounding enamel/dentin was lost to acid erosion, so the restoration stands high; investigate GI reflux and dietary acids rather than replacing or raising VDO first.
A periodontal probe with three alternating color bands (typically 3 mm increments) is the Marquis color-coded probe; other listed items are unrelated instruments or diagnoses.
Midface hypoplasia with exophthalmos (shallow orbits) and craniofacial deformity points to a craniosynostosis syndrome; among given choices only Apert syndrome fits that phenotype.
Dental sources of cavernous sinus thrombosis classically spread from maxillary anterior/canine infections via the infraorbital/angular venous pathway into the cavernous sinus.
Maxillary sinusitis commonly refers pain to posterior maxillary teeth with a non-odontogenic exam; among listed systemic infections it is the most frequent dental-pain mimic.
A chronic midpalatal ulcerative defect that perforates into the nose is classic for a tertiary syphilitic gumma; TB and osteomyelitis are less typical for that exact midline pattern.

Dark intrinsic stains need opaque, thin, durable bonded ceramic; etched porcelain veneers mask discoloration better than translucent composite and better than thick zirconia veneers in many board keys.

Contaminated instruments held briefly before sterilization should be kept moist in an appropriate holding/disinfectant or enzymatic solution so debris does not dry on; plain water or dry storage is inferior.
After non-surgical therapy, residual deep pockets with horizontal bone loss are treated surgically primarily to reduce pocket depth and improve cleanability, not merely to increase compliance.
When multiple reasonable plans exist (save tooth vs extract/implant), after informed consent on risks, prognosis, and cost, the final choice is driven by patient preferences and values.

Buccal cortical radiopaque laminated/periosteal lines with a chronically infected tooth and swelling suggest proliferative periostitis of osteomyelitis (e.g., Garre-type) rather than primary osteosarcoma.
Multiple myeloma is a malignant clonal proliferation of plasma cells in bone marrow; lymphocytes and macrophages are not the defining neoplastic cell.
Asking the patient to smile, close the eyes tightly, and wrinkle the forehead tests muscles of facial expression—cranial nerve VII (facial)—not trigeminal sensory motor only.

With a truncated stem (sore throat, low-grade fever) and VZV among herpesviruses, board keys often target varicella-zoster when vesicular/systemic viral illness is implied; HSV/EBV remain differential without more detail.

In untreated/poorly controlled hyperthyroidism, epinephrine-containing local anesthetic can precipitate a thyrotoxic crisis (agitation, dizziness, autonomic surge); the cause is thyrotoxicosis, not infection.
Unilateral painful itchy facial rash with blisters in a dermatome is herpes zoster (reactivation of VZV), not primary HSV (usually oral-labial clusters) or non-vesicular sinus disease.

Apexification of an immature nonvital tooth uses materials that induce a hard-tissue apical barrier; among the listed options, tricalcium phosphate (ceramic-type) is the only plausible apexification material.
Zinc oxide–eugenol cements show relatively high short-term solubility and disintegration in the oral environment compared with resin or glass-ionomer-based materials.
Multiple small yellowish submucosal spots on the lip mucosa are Fordyce granules—ectopic sebaceous glands—not sialadenitis or malignancy.
Among salivary-type malignancies listed, mucoepidermoid carcinoma is the most common malignant salivary gland tumor overall (including minor glands of the lip region in exam banks).
NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen) can trigger bronchospasm in aspirin-sensitive/severe asthma; paracetamol (acetaminophen) is the preferred first-line analgesic in that setting.
For an anxious patient with asthma and benzodiazepine allergy, nitrous oxide–oxygen provides titratable anxiolysis without relying on benzodiazepines or general anesthesia.
Internal (inflammatory) root resorption requires vital pulp tissue at the resorptive front; an intact predentin/odontoblast barrier helps protect mineralized dentin from clastic attack.
A full-coverage hard nightguard for bruxism should provide even occlusal contacts on both anterior and posterior teeth to distribute load and stabilize the appliance.
Classic nicotinic stomatitis (white-gray palate with red puncta in a smoker) is a reactive, usually benign change; reassure, counsel cessation, and monitor—biopsy only if atypical or nonresolving.

With few remaining strategic teeth (#17, 33, 43, 44), preserving roots/abutments for an overdenture improves retention, proprioception, and ridge preservation versus extracting everything for conventional CDs.
Horizontal mobility of about 1 mm faciolingually corresponds to Miller/Grade II mobility (≈1 mm horizontal movement); Type/Grade I is the first detectable mobility short of that range.
Proximal surfaces not reached by cups or wheels are polished with dental tape (or floss) charged with fine prophy paste to clean and polish contact areas safely.
Standard excisional biopsy uses an elliptical (fusiform) incision with length about 3× width so the wound can be closed primarily with minimal tension and dog-ears.
With a truncated stem asking which site/type for biopsy, the lip is a classic accessible site for minor salivary or mucosal lesions when that is the only coherent anatomic option listed.
